data.title: Westport Fuel Systems: Burning The Furniture To Heat The House - Sell
data.description:
Note:

I have covered Westport Fuel Systems Inc. or "Westport" (NASDAQ:WPRT) previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

Westport Fuel Systems Overview

On Monday, Westport Fuel Systems reported Q4 and full-year 2024 results, with revenues coming in ahead of expectations and surprisingly strong cash generation:

The company generated a whopping $22.6 million in free cash flow in the fourth quarter and ended the year with $37.6 million in cash and cash equivalents, as well as $33.7 million in debt.

However, increased cash generation was mainly a result of lower working capital requirements and proceeds received from the transfer of Westport's High Pressure Direct Injection ("HPDI") operations to a recently established joint venture ("Cespira") with key customer AB Volvo or "Volvo" (OTCPK:VLVLY, OTCPK:VOLAF, OTCPK:VOLVF).

Subsequent to year-end, the company collected an $11.4 million holdback receivable from Cummins Inc. (CMI) which covered extended warranty obligations related to the dissolved Cummins Westport joint venture.

Unfortunately, management does not expect the recent improvement to continue, as evidenced by the company's surprise going concern warning:

Based on our projected capital expenditures, debt servicing obligations and operating requirements under our current business plan, we are projecting that our cash and cash equivalents will not be sufficient to fund our operations through the next twelve months from the date of the issuance of this MD&A.

These conditions raise substantial doubt about Westport's ability continue as a going concern within one year after the date our December 31, 2024 Consolidated Financial Statements are issued.

To alleviate the concern, Westport has agreed to divest its core Light-Duty operations, which represented 87% of the company's sales last year:

Please note that the Light-Duty business was Westport's sole profitable segment. Going forward, the company will focus on its remaining Heavy-Duty OEM and High Pressure Controls & Systems operations:

This decision is expected to enable us to concentrate fully on providing affordable solutions for hard to decarbonize mobility applications like long haul and heavy-duty trucking that can take advantage of the unique, practical and affordable HPDI technology and our world class high-pressure components and systems technologies and scalable alternative fuel solutions, ensuring that we remain at the forefront of emissions-reducing innovations that are cost effective.

However, with Cespira being an unconsolidated entity and the High Pressure Controls & Systems segment representing less than 3% of total revenues in 2024, the remaining Westport Fuel Systems will be a tiny, unprofitable stub. Please note also that the company's transitional services agreement with Cespira is scheduled to expire at the end of Q2/2026 which will effectively reduce Heavy-Duty OEM segment revenues to zero.

Moreover, Cespira will likely require additional capital contributions over time.

While the buyer of the Light-Duty operations will assume approximately $27 million in related debt, the purchase price also includes the segment's (undisclosed) cash balances.

Assuming $20 million of cash being part of the Light-Duty segment, Westport's post-transaction cash balance would calculate to approximately $100 million. According to statements made by management on the conference call, the company would retain approximately $7 million in debt outstanding under its credit facility with Export Development Canada.

However, with the remaining business likely to generate substantially negative free cash flow for the foreseeable future, market participants are likely to assign a substantially negative enterprise value going forward.

Fellow Canadian alternative energy play Ballard Power Systems (BLDP) is experiencing the very same issue, as the company is currently trading at a more than 40% discount to its estimated cash balance at the end of Q1.

Depending on the company's cash usage going forward, Westport's runway might have been extended by several years:

However, cash burn could increase if management decides to pursue inorganic growth.

With large parts of the company having been sold in recent quarters, and prospects for the remaining operations being muted at best, I expect shares to mark new multi-year lows sooner rather than later.

Consequently, I am downgrading Westport Fuel Systems' common shares from "Hold" to "Sell".

Risk Factors

Quite frankly, I am struggling to come up with credible near-term catalysts for the stock other than a surprise move into one of the market's recent hype themes (Space, AI, Defense, Energy Storage, Nuclear, Crypto) but in the current environment, market participants are not likely to blindly chase shares of the latest free rider company.

Bottom Line

While Westport Fuel Systems reported reasonably good fourth quarter results, the company's going concern warning caught investors flat-footed.

To alleviate the concern, Westport has agreed to sell its core Light-Duty operations, which contributed the vast majority of the company's sales in recent years.

While net proceeds could help Westport Fuel Systems staying afloat well into the next decade, uncertainties regarding the company's ability to essentially start from scratch are likely to result in market participants assigning a substantially negative enterprise value to the remaining business for the time being.

With shares likely to mark new multi-year lows in the near future, I am downgrading Westport Fuel Systems' common shares from "Hold" to "Sell".

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Billionaire Investor Warns Of 'Significant Correction' And Buys High Yield: Our Picks
data.description:
Steve Cohen - Billionaire investor, New York Mets baseball team owner, and founder of Point72 Asset Management hedge fund - recently discussed his increasing bearishness on both the broader economy and the stock market itself. Additionally, he recently signaled his bullishness on the private credit space and, as a result, launched a new investment strategy at his hedge fund into the high-yield lending space. In this article, we will discuss further why Mr. Cohen is bearish on the economy and market, yet bullish on private credit, and then share some of our top picks in the high-yield private credit space right now in light of the recently souring economic outlook.

Why Steven Cohen Is Bearish On The Market & Bullish On High Yield

The reason Mr. Cohen is bearish on the market and the broader economy in general right now is due primarily to the impact of tariffs, slowing immigration, and government spending cuts. As he stated in a recent interview,

Tariffs cannot be positive, okay? I mean, it’s a tax. On top of that, we have slowing immigration, which means the labor force will not grow as rapidly as it has over the last five years.

Meanwhile, in reference to the effect of government spending cuts, he said,

When that money has been coursing through the economy over many years, now potential will be reduced or stopped in many ways. It’s got to be negative for the economy.

As a result, he thinks that the U.S. economy will likely slow its growth in the second half of the year, which could lead to a material pullback in the stock market. He stated,

My view [on the U.S. economy and market] is negative for the first time in a while, and I think we’re seeing the regime shift a little bit. It may only last a year or so, but it’s definitely a period where I think the best gains have been had. It wouldn’t surprise me to see a significant correction."

At the same time, he said, "I don’t think it’s going to be a disaster."

What this all points to is a period of likely stagflation, in which inflation remains higher for longer and growth slows meaningfully, weakening stocks that are susceptible to inflation and or slowing growth. This is particularly relevant given that the market appears to remain expensive despite the recent pullback in the S&P 500 (SPY) and Nasdaq (QQQ) to begin 2025, as numerous valuation models indicate.

This, in turn, helps to explain some of the bullishness that Stephen Cohen has on private credit, in addition to the fact that he believes demand significantly exceeds supply in the private credit space.

The reason why private credit is not a bad place to be investing right now—and which likely explains, at least in part, why Mr. Cohen has recently launched a new investment fund in the space at his hedge fund—is because private credit typically enjoys senior secured status in the companies to which the loans are made. This provides contractual returns that have a pretty good chance of achieving considerable recovery of the principal even if the borrower defaults. Additionally, these loans are typically small enough in nature that it is not too difficult to build a well-diversified portfolio of them, which further reduces the risks associated with the strategy. Moreover, the returns are quite high, especially when a prudent amount of leverage is applied, leading to double-digit returns on equity in most cases.

On top of that, the loans in private credit are often indexed to short-term interest rates and float off of them, thereby making them fairly inflation-resistant products. Given that higher inflation tends to produce higher interest rates, in an environment where inflation is elevated, these loans tend to achieve higher net interest spreads. Therefore, private credit can provide an attractive risk-adjusted return in a stagflationary environment, since the returns on equity are similar to those of common equity while enjoying greater defensiveness due to broad diversification and the senior secured status of the loans—along with a yield on those loans that actually increases with interest rates. This contrasts with many other products that pay high yields, such as REITs (VNQ), utilities (XLU), and infrastructure businesses (UTF), which tend to generate lower returns when interest rates rise.

Our Top Picks

With this outlook for the economy, market, and private credit space in view, how are we approaching our portfolio? Like Mr. Cohen, we think that the market is currently significantly overvalued and likely due for underperformance in the near term, especially if there is additional economic turbulence caused by Trump’s tariffs, even though we do think that government spending cuts are a net positive for the economy over the long term. At the same time, we also think that private credit offers an attractive risk-adjusted place to allocate capital right now. That being said, many publicly traded private credit companies such as BDCs (BIZD) like Ares Capital (ARCC), Main Street Capital (MAIN), and Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL) trade at premium valuations right now. Therefore, we do not think that they present compelling value.

However, there are some better opportunities right now, like Kayne Anderson BDC (KBDC) and Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (MSDL), which have virtually their entire portfolios invested in senior secured loans, are well diversified, and have prudently structured balance sheets. This makes them fairly defensive plays in the current environment, while at the same time trading roughly in line or even at a slight discount to their net asset values. As a result, they present much more attractive value for investors than some of the bigger names in this space. As a result, we are focusing our portfolio exclusively on these opportunities instead.

Investor Takeaway

As this article points out, the U.S. economy is facing uncertainty as the Trump administration’s push for massive new tariffs is likely going to put a strain on the economy—at least in the near term. Just how significantly it will impact the economy has yet to be determined, but when combining the potential headwinds from tariffs—which even other major investors like BlackRock’s (BLK) CEO Larry Fink have said are leading to a slowdown in the economy—with short-term headwinds from government spending cuts and potential near-term economic disruptions from reduced illegal immigration, there is a very distinct possibility of a near-term economic slowdown or even a recession, along with an accompanying market pullback from its current richly valued state.

Over the long term, we have a more positive view of the economic impacts of some of these changes, but in the meantime, we are trying to become increasingly cautious and prudent in our portfolio disposition. Part of this is increasing our exposure to private credit instead of some of the more aggressive equity investment opportunities out there. However, even then, we are still being very careful and only investing in BDCs with portfolios that are almost entirely first-lien senior secured and that have strong underwriting, conservative balance sheets, and valuations that are at or below net asset value. For example, we recently purchased shares in several conservatively positioned BDCs - including KBDC in our Retirement Portfolio - at High Yield Investor.

data.title: SMA Solar Technology: Still A Buy, But More Conservative Outlook
data.description:
Since my last article on SMA Solar Technology (OTCPK:SMTGF)(OTCPK:SMTGY), although only a few months have passed, it feels like years from a geopolitical perspective. The company faces new challenges, such as the potential imposition of import tariffs in the US, but also new opportunities, such as Germany's expansive fiscal policy.

In this context, the inverter market, despite starting to show tepid signs of improvement, is still characterized by oversupply. Companies are struggling to sell off the large amount of products in inventory, leading to significant write-offs. SMA itself, in FY24, recorded an inventory impairment of €113 million, representing 7.4% of revenue.

Considering SMA strong position in the utility-scale inverter market and management's efforts to implement operating cost cuts, I confirm the "buy" rating for the company. However, the intensification of geopolitical risks and the persistence of oversupply in the inverter market signal rising uncertainty about its activities. For this reason, the new target price, determined by the DCF analysis, drops from €23.75 per share in October 2024 to €20.51.

Business Update

SMA's financial results in FY24 were extremely dependent on the Large Scale & Project Solutions segment, which posted 38.9% YoY revenue growth, accounting for 76.8% of total sales compared to 44.3% in FY23. The high utility-scale PV system demand in both Europe and the US, resulting from the steady decline in LCOE, boosted the growth of the business unit, positioning SMA as one of the leading players in string and centralized inverters. This trend is also reflected in the segment's EBIT margin, which grew from 12.28% in FY23 to 19.34% in FY24. This increase, although partly driven by greater scalability due to increased production, also indicates a significant imbalance between supply and demand for these products, allowing SMA to raise their selling prices.

A completely opposite picture emerges for the Home Solutions and C&I Solutions segments, which experienced a general slump in both revenue and margins. In FY24, the two business units experienced a 70.7% and 61.6% collapse in sales, respectively, resulting in EBIT margins deep in the red. The latter result was also hit by a significant inventory impairment in Q4 FY24, amounting to €44.6 million (plus €4.2 million in production line impairment) for the Home Solutions segment and €49.5 million for the C&I Solutions segment.

I believe that the main reason for this outcome lies in the market's preference for microinverters designed for distributed PV systems. Indeed, this product has certain characteristics that make it more suitable for installation on residential or industrial PV systems. They have a lower cost and are significantly smaller, which is essential given the limited area of a rooftop.

Furthermore, the IRA prioritized microinverters by providing an Advanced Manufacturing Tax Credit (45X) of up to $0.11/W, despite their lower efficiency compared to other types of inverters. This impact is evident when looking at the evolution of Enphase Energy's (ENPH) (specialized in microinverters) EBIT margin compared to SMA and SolarEdge (SEDG). Unlike its two competitors, Enphase recovered much faster from the oversupply condition and closed the second half of 2024 with a positive and growing EBIT margin.

One last consideration that might disadvantage SMA compared to Enphase concerns the location of its production sites. The tax credits introduced by the IRA favor and better compensate components produced within the US. For this reason, SMA, having limited manufacturing capacity in the US, faces a competitive disadvantage compared to domestic companies.

Restructuring Program

In response to the challenging market situation, especially in the Home Solutions and C&I Solutions segments, SMA's management has planned a corporate restructuring that aims for an EBIT improvement of €200 million by FY27, with an expected positive impact of approximately €80 million by FY25. To reach this goal, Olaf Heyden has been appointed as Chief Transformation and Operational Officer, temporarily until FY26. The plan includes an organization-wide restructuring, involving the merger of the two business units mentioned above, as well as various measures designed to increase operational efficiency:

Optimizing prices and sales volumes to increase revenue. Of all the planned measures, this appears to be the most speculative, with no clear disclosure of the actual strategy for its implementation.

Optimization of material costs through a renegotiation process with suppliers, including the search for cheaper alternatives.

Optimization of operating costs by reducing fixed expenses to provide more flexibility in the event of adverse economic conditions for the company. A lower incidence of fixed costs on total costs makes it easier to implement cuts when necessary. In detail, a reduction in indirect costs related to fleet, IT licenses, and office supplies is planned.

Staff downsizing. Optimizing the organizational structure by merging the Home Solutions and C&I Solutions segments, while simultaneously limiting new hires. Both measures are directed at reducing administrative costs.

Tailwind From German Fiscal Stimulus

In March 2025, Germany announced a massive €1 trillion fiscal stimulus package aimed at reviving growth after several years marked by a steady mild contraction of the economy. Behind this landmark decision, which includes a loosening of the constitutional debt brake, lies the desire to limit the factors that have adversely affected the German economy since pandemic, foremost among them the high cost of energy. For this reason, although a considerable part of the stimulus will be allocated to military spending as a consequence of Russia's aggressive military policy, a large share of the package will be directed toward infrastructure, ecological transition, and the modernization of productive capital.

Given the limited energy resource capacity in the country, solar, wind, nuclear, and hydrogen energy will play a key role in achieving greater energy independence. As SMA is one of Germany's largest companies in the sector, I believe it will be able to capitalize on funds aimed at improving the company's production capacity and efficiency. Additionally, these funds are expected to lead to increased demand for utility-scale PV systems, both in Germany and Europe, with a positive effect on inverter demand. For this reason, I consider this development a significant opportunity for SMA in the medium to long-term horizon.

Financial Update

In FY24, SMA reported revenue down 19.6% YoY, attributable to a 4.5% reduction in inverter output sold (19.5 GW vs 20.4 GW in FY23) and a drop in selling prices. The oversupply condition led to an inventory impairment of €113 million plus a €33 million provision for severance. These one-off costs negatively impacted margins and profitability, pushing EBIT and net income into deep negative territory.

Considering an inventory of €563 million, I believe it is likely that the company will incur additional asset impairments in FY25 as well. For this reason, I lowered the revenue and margin estimates for FY25 and FY26 compared to my previous article. Management released guidance projecting revenue in the range of €1.5 billion to €1.65 billion and a midpoint EBITDA of €90 million for FY25. The effects of the restructuring are expected to drive margins improvement through FY27.

The net cash position of €84 million as of December 2024, though sharply down from €283 million as of December 2023, should provide the company with enough cash to fund the €115 million in CAPEX planned for FY25. In addition, the company is likely to generate a positive OCF in FY25, aided by higher EBITDA and a reduction in NWC, which was €473 million as of December 2024.

Main Risks

The imposition of tariffs on electrical components or generalized tariffs on the EU would have a major negative impact on SMA's bottom line. The German company generated 40% of its FY24 revenue in the Americas segment. The region is also the largest contributor to the order backlog as of December 2024 with a 49% share.

The inverter market is characterized by high competition from Chinese companies and the increasing competitiveness of US companies as a consequence of the IRA. Although this may boost demand for utility-scale PV systems in the US, it also enhances the competitiveness of US inverter manufacturers, consequently weakening SMA's position, especially in the Home Solutions and C&I Solutions segments.

The ongoing oversupply in the inverter market, primarily in Europe, could lead to weak results for SMA in FY25 as well.

Inflation and high interest rates negatively affect demand for new utility-scale PV systems, hindering SMA's revenue growth.

Discounted Cash Flow

To attribute a valuation to SMA Solar Technology, I performed a three-stage DCF. I made an estimate of the expected values of revenue, EBIT margin, CAPEX and depreciation for FY25, FY26, FY27. I made a projection of these values for the period between FY28 and FY34, finally calculating the terminal value. This analysis returned a target price of €20.51 per share, which is about 35% higher than SMA current market price. To determine this value, I made the following assumptions:

Beta was obtained using the European unlevered beta of "Electrical Equipment" industry reported by Professor Aswath Damodaran, subsequently calculating levered beta based on SMA D/E ratio. A value of 1.17 was derived.

Risk-free rate of 3.39%, based on average of the 10-year government bonds of the countries where SMA operates.

Equity risk premium of 4.83% was obtained from the weighted average of the ERPs of the countries where SMA operates. Countries' ERPs are provided by Aswath Damodaran. Cost of equity of 9.05% derived from CAPM model.

WACC of 9.05%.

Growth rate (g) of 2%.

Takeaway

SMA Solar Technology holds a strong competitive position in the utility-scale PV system inverter market. In addition, the German fiscal stimulus could provide an expansion opportunity for the company, supporting higher revenue growth. On the other hand, the restructuring undertaken by management in FY24 will optimize operating costs, positively affecting margins as early as FY25. Despite these strengths, the business in which the company operates is exposed to several risks, such as tariffs and the loss of competitiveness in US of the Home Solutions and C&I Solutions segments due to the IRA. Considering the DCF result, which indicates a potential upside of more than 35%, I assign SMA a "buy" rating, despite lowering the target price from €23.75 per share (October 2024 article) to €20.51.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: It's Tariff Day! Time To 'Buy The News?'
data.description:
By Mike Larson

“Buy the rumor, sell the news.” It’s an old Wall Street saying you’ve probably heard a million times.

But now that it’s Tariff Day, should you do the opposite? Is it time to buy the news?

Think about it: We’ve been selling the rumor of higher tariffs and a worsening trade war for weeks. Ever since the markets peaked in February, the averages got smacked hard enough to push the S&P 500 into an “official” correction - down 10% from its peak. Many of the highest-flying Artificial Intelligence (AI) and related stocks fared even worse. Even the Roundhill Magnificent Seven ETF (MAGS) is now down around 14% on the year.

But look at the MoneyShow Chart of the Day here. It shows the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). We had a high-volume selloff in February and March. Then we had a re-test on Monday... and it was successful (for now at least). Specifically, we tagged the previous low, then mounted a high-volume reversal to the upside -- and recorded a slightly higher low in RSI.

SPY: Is This the Start of a Bottoming Process?

Data by YCharts

I recently wrote about what a bottoming process might look like. And you know what? The action over the last few days looks kind of like that.

It’s only a start. Maybe President Trump’s tariff plans will be so aggressive, the short-term technical action won’t matter and we’ll just give up the ghost. But maybe... just maybe... the best play is to buy the news here!

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Safety Is Key For ZEEKR
data.description:
Introduction

My thesis is that safety is key for Zeekr (NYSE:ZK).

Despite a May 2024 IPO, Zeekr is still mostly owned by Geely Auto (OTCPK:GELYF). In November, Zeekr announced transactions which give them a 51% interest in Lynk and Reuters said Lynk was valued at $2.5 billion at the time. The Lynk acquisition was completed in February. Per the 2024 20-F, there were 2,541,971,138 Zeekr shares as of December 31 and Geely Auto owns 1,668,996,860 of them through their wholly owned Luckview Group Limited subsidiary. The Zeekr brand vehicles sell for about RMB 300 thousand, while the Lynk brand cars are about RMB 200 thousand.

Li Auto (LI) and others use acronyms such as automatic emergency braking (“AEB”) and automatic emergency steering (“AES”). The G-Pilot system from Geely Auto and Zeekr includes a general automated evasion system (“G-AES”) and the name can be a bit confusing given the AES acronym from Li Auto and others.

At the time of this writing, 1,000 RMB is equivalent to about $138.`

Safety Emphasis

Per a May 2024 release from Euro NCAP, Zeekr vehicles perform well with respect to safety ratings. Euro NCAP and others are aware of how Advanced Driver-Assistance Systems (“ADAS”) make vehicles safer, and this will be a key consideration in the years ahead:

Congratulations to ZEEKR for producing two vehicles with such good safety and overall environmental performance just as they begin their journey into the European electric vehicle market. Their vehicles show that safety and sustainability can sit alongside each other in modern cars. More generally, Euro NCAP is committed to the promotion of ADAS systems which are neither distracting nor annoying to drivers.

The 2024 20-F talks about Zeekr’s AEB system, which can safely brake to avoid stationary obstacles at speeds of up to 120 km/h (emphasis added):

In August 2024, Zeekr Intelligent Driving 2.0 was launched, marking our entry into the AI large-model era. We also introduced the 720-degree Active Safety System, fully transitioning to in-house-developed active safety solutions while significantly enhancing performance and scenario coverage. The AEB system now supports emergency braking at speeds of up to 120 km/h for stationary vehicles and has been upgraded to G-AEB, enabling braking for general obstacles.

Many safety revelations came out during Zeekr’s March 18 G-Pilot Launch event. Geely’s G-Pilot was introduced a few weeks before the event, and it has five variants: H1, H3, H5, H7, and H9. Zeekr’s March 18 event covered features which will be installed in H7 and H9. Zeekr’s G-Pilot hardware has Orin-X chips with computing power of 508 trillion operations per second (“TOPS”). This is similar to what we see with XPeng and the Li Auto Max, although the Li Auto Pro is lower. It is said Tesla HW3 is about 144 TOPS, while HW4 is estimated to be about twice as powerful as HW4. BYD’s top A tier is about 600 TOPS, while their B is about 300 and their C is about 100.

Though it can be expensive, Zeekr believes laser radar is a necessity for ultimate safety. They show a situation where a Zeekr 007 is driving in a dark environment where there are no streetlights. The vehicle is going 100 km/hour and a car going the opposite direction flashes its high beams. The camera’s image is of limited use, but the radar sees the obstacle and an accident is avoided. As such, Zeekr says the laser radar is irreplaceable for high-end vehicles. Their goal is to protect every Zeekr user with ultimate safety, and they say it is the gene engraved in their bones as opposed to just a slogan.

Zeekr says their safety features have helped them avoid over 3.5 million collisions. They have video of their 001 model using G-AES repeatedly and the sound made when this happens is a dot, dot, dash sequence which can be repeated multiple times. They show another scenario where the cameras don’t see much due to light limitations, but the laser radar powers G-AES and accidents are avoided continuously.

The first advantage with G-AES is that it can work at speeds of up to 130km/hour, which is about 81 mph. The second advantage is its recognition capability, ranging from large objects down to small objects like a bottle of mineral water. Additionally, it can avoid objects like falling rocks, which can be almost identical in color to the surface area of the road. Thirdly, G-AES is flexible in that it can avoid objects continually.

One safety advantage stressed in the March 18 event is the data held by Geely/Zeekr (emphasis added):

We have the largest safety scenario database in the industry. And it is five times higher than the industry average. In this database, we have 30 years of real traffic accident data in China. And also, we have inherited Volvo's safety experiences. and today these have been integrated into our active and passive safety and intelligent driving solutions.

Valuation

One factor making Zeekr valuable in my view is the way they have future-proofed their battery architecture by going to 800v early. XPeng (XPEV) is also known for 800v, and I believe it will be a requirement in order to be competitive in the high-end BEV space in China in the years ahead. Others are starting to do this, and we see the new 2025 Seal from BYD (OTCPK:BYDDY) is also 800v. I believe Tesla (TSLA) may fall behind in this area as their only 800v model is the CyberTruck.

Obviously, the number of units Zeekr produces in the future has a big impact on their current valuation. We can make predictions about the future numbers based on what we’ve seen in the recent past. Again, Zeekr closed the Lynk acquisition in February so the numbers for that month below in this table from the 20-F are the sum of 14,039 Zeekr brand units plus 17,328 Lynk brand units. In March 2025, the group sold 15,422 Zeekr brand units and 25,293 Lynk brand units for a combined volume of 40,715 units:

One area of concern with respect to unit sales was January 2025. Excluding the Lynk brand, the Zeekr brand slipped in January 2025 when they sold 11,942 units, which was less than the 12,537 units from January 2024. Per the 4Q24 call, more than half a million Zeekr brand and Lynk brand vehicles were sold in 2024, which was nearly a 30% YoY increase. 222 thousand of the vehicles were Zeekr brand. For 2025, they are targeting a total of more than 710 thousand units, composed of 320 thousand Zeekr brand units and 390 thousand Lynk brand units.

In the 4Q24 call, management said one part of the growth strategy for 2025 is strengthening the Zeekr brand to go more upmarket, while they broaden the Lynk customer base and expand the portfolio. The three new Zeekr brand models planned for 2025 are the 007GT, the 9X and a large size SUV. The new Lynk models coming in 2025 are the 900 along with a large-size sedan. Combining the Zeekr and Lynk brands, the group sold 507 thousand units in 2024 and the goal for 2025 is 710 thousand units.

Battery and component sales have dropped from 32% of the total in 2022 down to 22% for 2024, and they are made up of motors and electric control system products provided by Ningbo Viridi. Combined revenue has gone up substantially from 31.9 billion in 2022 to RMB 51.7 billion in 2023 to RMB 75.9 billion in 2024:

The gross profit for the three years above was RMB 2.5 billion, RMB 6.9 billion and RMB 12.5 billion, respectively, while the net loss to shareholders was RMB 7.9 billion, RMB 8.3 billion and RMB 6.4 billion, respectively.

Driven by RMB 7.5 billion in notes payable, we have cash flow from operations (“OCF”) of RMB 3.2 billion for 2024. We subtract RMB 1.3 billion in capex and RMB 0.4 billion in intangibles to arrive at free cash flow (“FCF”) of RMB 1.5 billion. The financing section of the cash flow statement shows RMB 3.5 billion in proceeds from the IPO.

The balance sheet shows RMB 32.7 billion in total assets against RMB 42.8 billion in total liabilities, for a deficit of nearly RMB 10.2 billion (rounding). This is a scary balance sheet, especially for a company with operating losses!

Again, the Lynk acquisition didn’t close until February, so the above revenue numbers for 2024 exclude them. CFO Yuan Jing said the following in the 4Q24 call about the combined revenue of Zeekr and Lynk (emphasis added):

In February of this year, we successfully completed the acquisition of Lynk & Co, and we have disclosed the pro forma condensed combined financial data for ZEEKR Group the year 2023 and 2024. As shown in the combined pro forma financial results in 2024, the combined ZEEKR Group achieved a remarkable revenue milestone of RMB 130.9 billion, backed by vehicle deliveries of more than 507,000 cars.

Given the scary balance sheet and the operating losses, I only value Zeekr at a fraction of their combined pro forma revenue of nearly RMB 131 billion. I think a valuation of about one-third the RMB 131 billion pro forma revenue may be reasonable. I think in terms of ranges, so +/ 10% sounds right. As such, my valuation range is RMB 39 billion to nearly RMB 48 billion or $5.4 to $6.6 billion.

Per the IPO announcement, each ADS represents 10 ordinary shares. Again, there were 2,541,971,138 shares as of December 31 per the 2024 20-F. Dividing by 10 and multiplying by the March 25 ADS price of $23.97 gives us a market cap of $6.1 billion. The market cap is inside my valuation range and I view the stock as a hold.

Forward-looking investors should keep an eye out for the Shanghai auto show in late April, where Zeekr will talk about their level 3 self-driving tech. Additionally, the new Zeekr brand 9X Grand flagship SUV PHEV will be discussed at the show.

Disclaimer: Any material in this article should not be relied on as a formal investment recommendation. Never buy a stock without doing your own thorough research.

data.title: Service Corporation: Benefiting From Aging Population; Strong Buy
data.description:
Service Corporation International (NYSE:SCI) offers funeral and cemetery services in the North American markets, accounting for around 17% of total market share. Coming out of the global pandemic, Service Corporation is well positioned to capture the business recovery in the funeral market. I think the potential corporate tax cut could be a potential catalyst for its stock price. I am initiating with a ‘Strong Buy’ rating with a fair value of $98 per share.

During the Covid period, Service Corporation experienced accelerated growth, with 14.4% of adjusted comparable funeral growth and 23.4% of comparable cemetery sales increase FY21.

An estimated of 110,000 funeral services were pulled-forward from 2020 to 2022 caused by the global pandemic, according to the last CMD. In the post-pandemic period, their funeral volume started to decline, as shown in the table below. After a three-year decline, I think the company has reached a reflection point where volume will start to normalize. In addition, the company has been actively expanding its cemetery through organic growth and M&A. Its comparable cemetery revenue has delivered robust growth over the past two years, rising 1.9% in FY23, and 3% in FY24.

I favor Service Corporation’s business for the following reasons:

Firstly, Service Corporation’s future growth will benefit from an aging population, as more people will likely become the company’s at-need and preneed customers. As depicted in the chart below, the funeral and cemetery business is expected to be driven by the post-baby boomer generation. The demographic trend will create a long-term growth tailwinds for Service Corporation.

Secondly, Service Corporation operates predominantly in the US, generating more than 90% of revenue domestically. As shown in the map below, the company has an extensive network for their funeral and cemetery services. The strong penetration in the US market could enable the company to improve sales productivity, create network effects and optimize its cost structure. During the Covid period, many small regional funeral homes struggled to provide adequate services to local communities due to labor shortages. In contrast, Service Corporation could reallocate their staff across different regions and remain operational during the crisis.

Lastly, I think the potential corporate tax cut could benefit Service Corporation. If the US government cut the federal tax rate from 21% to 15%, I estimate the company’s net income could increase by 14%. If US-centric business nature could maximize the benefits of the potential tax cut.

The company reported its Q4 FY24 result on February 12th, delivering 4% revenue growth and 14% adjusted EPS growth. As depicted in the chart below, the comparable revenue growth in preneed cemetery and funeral has experienced some signs of stabilization, increasing by 2% and 2.7% year-over-year, respectively.

Its funeral volume declined by 4.4% year-over-year, while the core average revenue per service improved by 2.7%, demonstrating a robust pricing environment. Looking forward, I anticipate the funeral volume will normalize, as the company has already experienced three years of post-pandemic decline.

The company is guiding for adjusted EPS of $3.85 at the midpoint for FY25, implying 9% year-over-year growth. For the near-term growth, I forecast its funeral business will grow by 4%, assuming 2% normalized volume growth and 2% price growth aligned with inflation. In addition, I anticipate its cemetery revenue will increase by 6% annually, driven by strong preneed sales. The management has prioritized the core preneed sale production as a key KPI for their salesforce. As discussed before, the aging population could create a long-term tailwind for the cemetery growth. Putting together, I calculate the total revenue will grow by 5% organically. I forecast the company will allocate 4% of revenue toward M&A, aligned with its historical trends, which could contribute an additional 1.1% growth to the topline, as my estimates.

As shown in the chart below, Service Corporation has significantly reduced its total shares outstanding in recent years, through share repurchases. I expect the company to continue shrinking its share count by 3%, assuming it will allocate 6% of revenue toward shares buyback, aligned with historical trends.

I forecast the company will expand its operating margin by 20bps, driven by price increase and improvement of sales productivities. Service Corporation has trained its sales to obtain insurance licenses and transition from trust to insurance-funded preneed contracts, as noted in the earnings call. These initiatives could potentially improve its sales productivities and boost the average sales per contract, in my opinion.

The DCF can be summarized as follows:

The WACC is calculated to be 8.8%, assuming: risk-free rate 4.2%; beta 0.9; equity risk premium 7%; cost of debt 5%; equity $11.5 billion; debt $4.8 billion; tax rate 23%. After discounting all the future FCFF with a 4% terminal growth rate, the total enterprise value is calculated to be $18 billion. After making some adjustments, as shown in the table below, the fair value is estimated to be $98 per share.

Risks

  • The Funeral and cemetery industry is a very competitive and fragmented market, with many small players in the US market. I believe the funeral business has moderate barriers to entry. Additionally, some large private equity firms have been actively acquiring and consolidating businesses, intensifying the industry competition.
  • During the recent conference, the CFO noted that the company remained actively in the M&A market; however, he was disappointed with the valuation levels. The company spent $180 million on acquiring funeral homes and cemetery real estates in FY24. Service Corporation’s future growth relies on a healthy M&A pipeline to further consolidate the fragmented market.
  • The Canadian operations represent approximately 5% of total revenue. The weak CAD relative to USD would pose some revenue growth and margin headwinds for Service Corporation.

In short, Service Corporation operates a resilient business model, with potential catalysts from an aging population and potential tax cut. I am initiating with a ‘Strong Buy’ rating on Service Corporation, with a fair value of $98 per share.

data.title: 13% Yield, +7% Dividend Hike: Annaly Capital
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

I fear all we have done is to awaken a sleeping giant and fill him with a terrible resolve – Admiral Isoroku Yamamoto

The admiral responsible for planning the attack on Pearl Harbor for the Japanese was claimed to have penned this in his journal after Roosevelt's address to Congress. The attack on Pearl Harbor was horrendously effective, but not in the methodology that the Japanese had hoped for. The attack on Pearl Harbor was horrendously effective at bringing the entire American people together in their focus and resolve to defeat Japan. Japan had hoped the attack on Pearl Harbor would stop America from being effective in stopping their expansion within the Pacific. But in the end, it helped bring about the end of Japan's Imperial stage. America was considered to be the sleeping giant because it had considerable resources and population that had not yet been turned to the war effort. America had largely been a supplier of arms and ammunitions, but not an active combatant. It was after this time that America fully joined the war effort, both in Europe and in the Pacific, and engaged itself in the focus of winning the war.

In the market, there can be sleeping giants as well. Companies or sectors that benefit in times of significant distress or economic weakness. These sleeping giants can be awoken by certain factors moving around the economy, allowing them to become the heroes in your portfolio when everything else seems to be falling apart and stumbling.

Today, I'm going to take a moment to look at one of these investments that we've covered previously and show how this may be the time that it has finally awoken and becomes a hero in your portfolio, even when the rest of the holdings in your portfolio are showing signs of weakness.

Let's dive in!

A Sleeping Giant Awoken?

Annaly Capital Management, Inc. (NYSE:NLY), yielding 13.8%, hiked its dividend for the first time since 2010 – raising its dividend 7.7% to $0.70/quarter after 15 years of a dividend slowly trending downward.

We have often stated that NLY is a company that is capable of doubling its dividend during a recession. The reason is that NLY's core business is to invest in agency MBS (Mortgage-Backed Securities). Agency MBS is an asset that doesn't carry credit risk. If a borrower defaults, the agency – Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac – will buy back the mortgage at par. As a result, agency MBS is an asset class that does best when fear is high and investors are looking for safe havens. When the stock market is crashing because of a recession, agency MBS is usually shining.

We were pleasantly surprised by the timing of NLY's hike, as we were expecting them to maintain throughout the year based on this exchange in the earnings call:

Bose George

Okay, great. And then just in terms of an update on the dividend, just given the run rate of earnings, I guess it's fair to say the dividend looks well covered.

David Finkelstein

Yes, I think we feel that for 2025, the dividend certainly feels safe and it's obviously a conversation we have every quarter with our Board and as we evaluate the dividend, we always look for durability. So we don't take those decisions lightly and we go through a thorough evaluation. We feel good about where things are at and our outlook is optimistic that we'll be able to maintain this run rate.

Apparently, after the earnings call, management's optimism increased as their outlook went from maintaining to hiking. We saw a similar hike from another agency mREIT we follow, Dynex (DX).

This change in outlook is likely due to a combination of NLY trading above book value, which allows it to raise equity to buy MBS. After NLY reported Q4 earnings in January, their price started trading over book value consistently. At the same time, MBS prices went through a dip in January and early February, trading at the lowest prices since April 2024. Here is a look at 5.5% coupon MBS over the past year: Source

We expect that NLY was likely busy raising capital with their ATM program, allowing them to use new capital to buy the dip. They also might have decided to increase leverage. One of the largest reasons agency mREITs have been cautious about increasing their asset base is that there was still a level of uncertainty about whether or not the Fed might hike rates one more time. The dip in MBS prices created the perfect storm where NLY could be very confident that the Fed isn't going to hike rates anytime soon; their equity was trading above book value, allowing them to raise new capital, and MBS prices had declined significantly from recent highs. NLY likely saw this as a signal to buy, and we expect when we see NLY's Q1 2025 earnings we will see that they raised equity, increased the size of their portfolio, and possibly increased their leverage.

A larger portfolio translates to higher earnings, and knowing that they had that buying opportunity, management, and the Board decided to increase the dividend.

Historically, when NLY started hiking, its dividend didn't just go up once. One hike was followed by several hikes. From 2000-2002, NLY's dividend went from $1.00/quarter to $2.72/quarter. From 2005-2009, NLY's dividend bottomed at $0.40/quarter and peaked at $3.00/quarter. All those numbers are adjusted for NLY's reverse split.

This cycle, NLY's dividend bottomed at $0.65/quarter. If we see a recession, the dividend could go up a lot. A dividend increase of 3-5x would be consistent with what NLY has experienced in past recessions.

Previously, once NLY started hiking, the raises kept coming quickly. There is a lot about this interest rate cycle that has been far slower than we saw from 2000-2010, so this time the raises might come slower. Although, if the U.S. economy tips into recession, that will almost certainly speed up the process.

For the past several years, we've been left wondering where the bottom would be for the MBS markets as the Fed hiked rates very quickly but has been very slow to bring them back down.

It is now becoming increasingly clear that MBS prices bottomed in late 2023:

With dividend hikes starting to happen throughout the sector, it is also likely that the bottom is in for cash flow for agency mREITs. We are getting over 13% yield with dividend growth more likely than not. Additionally, if there is a recession, we could see very substantial dividend growth. 300-500% would be consistent with NLY's historical experience. We can't know if this cycle will be that high or not, but what we can be sure of is that if a recession is going to hit the U.S. economy, we want to own NLY.

Conclusion

NLY can be an investment that confuses many investors, especially investors who have entered the market when the stock market was in the longest-running bull market ever. They can look at an investment like this, pull up their chart, simply see a chart that they don't understand, and reject it out of hand, moving on to something else. I can respect if they are unwilling to do the research and to dive deeper into an investment; avoiding it because they don't understand it can be a beneficial choice.

However, an income investor should not be one who simply avoids investments instead of doing deeper research and developing a deeper understanding. The ability to find wonderful income in the market requires effort. The goal is to put the effort in upfront so that you can collect wonderful passive income on the other side. It is where you don't need to constantly fiddle or adjust your portfolio, but you can sit back and relax and enjoy your retirement that is covered in layers upon layers of income, which is pouring in from the market.

Many investors choose the opposite route. They choose the lazy option up front, investing in a market-wide passive ETF (SPY)(VOO), and then they have to be more active in selling their shares, and they have their stress and worry at the end – when they should be enjoying a retirement filled with ease and relaxation. Whenever someone tells me that being a dividend investor is probably more stressful initially than being a passive market ETF investor, I would probably agree. But the goal isn't to have no stress now; the goal is to have no stress in the future, when you're retired, and you're enjoying the benefits of your hard work.

If you're in retirement, putting in a little bit of effort now so that you can enjoy the rest of your retirement and ease and luxury is well worth doing the work instead of having to pay the piper later in the stress and worry of having to choose when to sell your shares to live and hope your nest egg doesn't burn out before you do. NLY can play an important role in this process by being a strong income producer when the rest of your portfolio may be struggling.

data.title: SCHD: Great Dividend Growth, Excellent For Tough Times
data.description:
Article Thesis

Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:SCHD) is a high-quality income-focused ETF that offers a reliable dividend yield, compelling sector diversification, and below-average volatility. For uncertain times like right now, this is an attractive combination, I believe.

Past Coverage

I have written about Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently in the summer of 2024, around nine months ago. I was bullish back then, which has worked out well so far -- despite a bit of a pullback in recent months, SCHD has delivered total returns of 10% since my last article was published, easily outpacing the broad market's (as measured by the S&P 500 (SPY)) 3% return over the same time frame.

Despite Uncertain Times, Dividends Are Growing

Investors have different goals -- for many, income is a key goal. This includes retirees and other investors who live off their dividends, but even among those who are not (yet) living off their portfolio cash flow, there are many investors who like the income investing approach -- I belong to that group of investors. This is due to the fact that dividends can be a significant total return driver while receiving a reliable flow of income from our portfolio can also help us stomach harsh times more easily -- selling following or during a market crash can be a huge psychologically caused error, and a nice income stream helps us avoid it, as we will want to hold on to our dividend-paying stocks even during tough times.

Income investments can be especially attractive when they manage to grow their dividends over time. For single stocks, that is a pretty straightforward thing -- when the company raises its dividend, our dividend stream will grow, even if we do not buy any additional shares. When it comes to an ETF like SCHD, things are a bit more complicated, as some of the companies will increase their dividends in a specific time frame, while other portfolio companies won't. Some may even reduce their dividends. Also, SCHD's management is buying and selling stocks from time to time, so the portfolio is not constant -- it changes over time. Add the fact that some companies do not pay quarterly, but monthly, annually, or semi-annually (with special dividends on top of that in some cases), and the portfolio cash flow that SCHD generates can vary a bit from quarter to quarter, which translates into some minor ups and downs in SCHD's dividend over time. But when we look at a longer time frame, things are looking great:

In the above table from Seeking Alpha, we see that Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF's dividend grade is excellent, at A+. When we delve into the details, things look great as well, with SCHD showing double-digit dividend growth rates over the last year, the last 3 years, the last five years, and the last decade. Despite the fact that dividends can decline on a quarter-to-quarter basis from time to time, they have grown on a year-over-year basis for more than a decade. The dividend growth rate over the last year was significantly higher than the longer-term average, which naturally looks good -- this may be a sign of dividend growth accelerating going forward, but that is not guaranteed. The good news is that even if dividend growth were to decline back to the ~11% range it has stood at over the last decade, that would still be highly attractive. After all, SCHD offers a starting dividend yield of 3.7% at current prices -- the combination of a dividend yield of close to 4% and a dividend growth rate in the 11% range would suggest that an annual return of 15% is possible, assuming the dividend yield stays flat over time (i.e. share prices rise in line with dividends).

The combination of a solid starting yield and strong dividend growth also will result in massive income being generated down the road, as the following scenarios show:

- If SCHD grows its dividend at 11% per year for 5 years, with a starting yield of 3.7% and all dividends being reinvested over those 5 years, the yield on cost for someone buying today would be 7.5% five years from now.

- If SCHD grows its dividend by 11% per year for the next ten years, with a 3.7% starting dividend yield and with dividends being reinvested along the way, the yield on cost would jump to a hefty 15%.

There is, of course, no guarantee that SCHD will maintain its dividend growth rate -- but the consistency of the dividend growth rate over the last 3, 5, and 10 years suggests that the chance of that happening isn't low. But even if dividend growth were to average just 7.3% per year over the next decade -- two-thirds of the 11% historic average -- then the yield on cost for someone buying today could be 11% a decade from now.

SCHD continues to have a strong dividend growth track record, with dividend growth even accelerating in the recent past, despite a wide range of macro troubles including military conflicts, trade tensions, growth worries in major economies such as China and Europe, and so on.

SCHD: The Right Choice For Uncertain Times

With tariff tensions between the US and many other economies being high, the market has been volatile in recent weeks. And this could continue going forward, I believe, due to uncertainties around trade, economic growth, inflation, interest rate movements, and so on.

During such uncertain times, Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF could be a good choice: Not only does it offer steadily growing income, but it also is not very volatile. Its beta stands at 0.83, suggesting that it will move less compared to the broad market during sell-offs or market downturns. We have seen this over the last couple of weeks:

The broad market dropped 6% over the last month, largely driven by the even bigger sell-off in tech stocks, which have a huge weight in the S&P500. SCHD, however, declined by just 2%, slipping less than half as much as the broad market. The fund's diversification across different industries helps with that, especially since some of the fund's top sectors, such as healthcare or consumer staples, are not very cyclical. SCHD's management also tries to emphasize businesses that are resilient and low-risk, which helps reduce risks and volatility for investors that hold SCHD.

If the European Union wants to "strike back" at US companies, the tech mega-caps could be a relatively easy target. The fact that SCHD is not very exposed to the tech industry could thus be positive in case trade tensions between the EU and the US escalate -- the broad market, and especially the NASDAQ index (QQQ), would most likely be hit a lot harder than SCHD.

Is SCHD An Attractive Investment?

Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF holds large, high-quality, and, I would say, relatively low-risk companies such as AbbVie (ABBV), Coca-Cola (KO), Texas Instruments (TXN), and BlackRock (BLK). Its approach of finding the best values in its investment universe has allowed for compelling results in the past, both when it comes to growing its dividend reliably and quickly over time, as well as when it comes to generating total returns -- those stand at 13% per year since inception. With an expense ratio of just 0.06% and strong liquidity (15 million shares are traded per day), SCHD's "ETF data" looks quite good as well.

With its sector diversification, its exposure to non-cyclical, recession-resilient businesses, its below-average volatility, and its reliable and growing dividend stream, SCHD is an appealing investment overall, but it could be especially valuable during tough times. While it is possible that equity markets recover quickly from the recent sell-off, it is also possible that waters will remain choppy due to a wide range of macro uncertainties and troubles -- and SCHD would be a good pick for such a scenario, I believe.

data.title: Why HealthTech? Why HEAL?
data.description:
The healthcare industry faces a pivotal moment as traditional care models struggle to meet growing demand. While preventive approaches have improved chronic condition management and patient outcomes, the gap between healthcare supply and demand continues to expand. Technology is becoming the essential connection across this divide, with the healthtech market expected to grow dramatically from about $225 billion in 2022 to over $1 trillion by 2032.1

This expansion is expected to encompass four key areas: healthcare analytics and software solutions, technology-enabled consumer healthcare, smart medical devices, and AI-enabled drug discovery. Each area represents a unique approach to expanding provider capacity and improving care delivery efficiency. While healthcare has historically been cautious in adopting new technologies, mounting pressure to serve an aging population, manage chronic conditions,2 and improve access to care is accelerating digital transformation across the industry.

In response to these trends, we’ve updated the Global X Telemedicine & Digital Health ETF (EDOC) to the Global X HealthTech ETF (HEAL), replacing the index the fund seeks to track effective April 1, 2025. This change reflects the rapidly expanding healthtech landscape and broadens our investment scope in an effort to capture more opportunities across the entire digital health ecosystem. HEAL seeks to provide investors with comprehensive exposure to companies driving healthcare’s technological revolution, positioning them to potentially benefit from the industry’s projected growth.

AI-Enabled Drug Discovery

Despite technological advancements, developing a new medicine still takes 10–15 years and costs $1.3 billion on average.4,5 Only one in ten investigational drugs, however, makes it to market.6 By running millions of scenarios, AI software could reduce the cost of preclinical drug development by 20-40% as well as accelerate design and validation of drug candidates up to 15-fold.7,8

The drug development process is complex, requiring careful validation at each stage to ensure that only the most promising candidates advance to clinical trials and U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) review. A flawed assumption or connection made in the early stages can result in years of wasted development time and resources on a drug that ultimately proves ineffective for patients. AI technology offers a potential solution by enabling researchers to leverage vast amounts of health data to make more informed decisions from the very beginning of the drug development process. This data-driven approach helps identify potential issues earlier and increases the likelihood of selecting successful drug candidates.

AI drug discovery software companies like Schrodinger, Simulation Plus, and Absci are pioneering a rapidly expanding industry. These providers can rapidly evaluate billions of potential compounds and predict how molecules will behave in the body. Further, they can harness machine learning to analyze massive biomedical datasets, revealing previously hidden patterns in disease mechanisms and optimizing drug designs digitally before any physical testing occurs.

The AI-enabled drug discovery software sector is projected to be the fastest-growing generative AI segment through 2032, with a compound annual growth rate of 121%.9 HEAL offers exposure to such software providers.

This industry is, in turn, also expected to benefit:

  • Pharmaceutical Companies: The integration of AI could fundamentally improve the economics of drug development. Currently, pharmaceutical companies must invest in hundreds of drug candidates knowing that only a small fraction will reach the market. This means approved drug prices must account for both successful and failed development costs. With AI-enhanced success rates, companies could develop more drugs more efficiently, potentially leading to better returns on investment and more affordable medicines.
  • Technology Infrastructure Providers: The tech industry is making significant investments in healthcare AI capabilities. Chip manufacturers, in particular, are prioritizing healthcare development to meet growing drug discovery demands. For example, Nvidia has established partnerships with hundreds of pharmaceutical and genomic firms, with their healthcare division now generating an estimated $1 billion in annual revenue.10

Smart Medical Devices

Smart medical devices are being deployed across the healthcare system to help scale up supply. These often apply novel software and AI applications to innovative hardware to help fill gaps in care. The FDA has approved approximately 950 AI-enabled medical devices in the United States, a dramatic increase from just 6 approved devices a decade ago.3, 11

Smart medical wearables can bring healthcare technology directly to patients for continuous monitoring without sacrificing comfort. Diabetes devices from companies like Tandem Diabetes and Dexcom have paved the way in this category. Their success — evidenced by a 67% reduction in hospitalizations — creates a foundation for expanded monitoring across cardiovascular and neurological conditions.12 Multi-biomarker tracking devices now chart the course for wearable health technology across diverse therapeutic applications.

Surgical robots have also been increasingly expanding their use cases. Though surgical robots have been in use for over two decades, advances in technology and software integration now enable more complex procedures. Benefits include reduced hospital stays, minimal scarring, lower infection risks, and decreased post-operative pain.13 Accessibility has improved through alternative financing options, with leasing arrangements accounting for approximately 60% of placements by industry leader Intuitive Surgical.14 Since receiving the first surgical robot approval in 2000, Intuitive Surgical has expanded to nearly 10,000 installations and facilitates over 2.6 million annual procedures.15

Similar devices are also being deployed behind the scenes, perhaps where patients might not directly see them in action. Healthcare facilities are rapidly adopting behind-the-scenes automation technologies. In the expanding $90 billion pharmacy automation market, integrated dispensing systems are revolutionizing medication management. These systems – like Omnicell’s – can address critical demand as 85% of U.S. hospitals face pharmacy technician shortages.16 Modern solutions, such as Omnicell’s automated dispensing systems, deliver significant improvements – increasing inventory capacity by 30% and reducing pharmacist workload by 75%.17,18

Healthcare Analytics & Software Solutions

While medical technology has advanced rapidly in areas like genomics and surgical robots, fundamental administrative processes remain surprisingly analog. An estimated 80% of U.S. healthcare documentation still travels via fax and traditional mail.19 This disconnect between advanced medical capabilities and outdated administrative processes creates significant challenges for healthcare providers and patients alike:

  • Physicians spend nearly 40% of their time on documentation rather than patient care.20
  • Three-quarters of medical staff report that documentation requirements actively interfere with patient care.21
  • More than three-quarters of physicians complete their documentation outside of regular hours.22
  • Nearly 80% of doctors experience burnout related to healthcare IT systems.23

AI-powered solutions are now bridging this divide through intelligent workflow automation. Advanced natural language processing – like the software capabilities offered by Doximity – can help generate clinical documentation, from progress notes to discharge summaries. Enterprise content management platforms – like Veeva’s flagship Vault – can help manage regulatory documentation and clinical trial data. Patient recruitment and retention software for clinical trials – such as IQVIA’s CORE technology solutions – seek to support clinical trial enrollment, a critical need given that an estimated 80% of trials miss their enrollment deadlines.24 AI chatbots – like Tempus’ innovative Tempus One platform – can instantly summarize relevant clinical research and genomic insights to help physicians make more informed treatment decisions for their patients.

These innovations extend beyond mere efficiency – they’re reshaping how the healthcare industry interacts with information.

The impact reaches beyond individual practices or businesses. By aggregating de-identified clinical data across diverse populations, these platforms create a living knowledge network. Providers can access insights from millions of patient encounters, stay current with emerging research, and make evidence-based decisions aligned with the latest standards of care. This collective intelligence, delivered through privacy-preserving analytics, is establishing a new foundation for medical decision-making.

Healthcare Analytics & Software Solutions

The rise of digital health solutions is fundamentally reshaping how patients access and experience healthcare. The global telemedicine market is projected to reach $280 billion by 2032, reflecting growing demand for convenient, accessible care options.25 While virtual care adoption accelerated during the COVID-19 pandemic, the sector has evolved far beyond basic video consultations.

Digital pharmacies are revolutionizing medication management, with platforms like Hims & Hers now serving over 2 million subscribers.26 These services combine home delivery with AI-powered medication management systems, reducing errors and improving adherence. Meanwhile, online healthcare marketplaces are streamlining how patients find and book care, significantly reducing wait times for specialist appointments.

Emerging hybrid care models blend virtual and in-person services, with a majority of healthcare organizations now offering some form of hybrid care delivery. These solutions particularly benefit chronic condition management, where continuous monitoring and regular provider check-ins can significantly improve outcomes. Remote patient monitoring programs have shown 50% reduction in hospital readmissions for conditions like heart failure and diabetes.27

Conclusion

The healthcare industry is experiencing unprecedented transformation through innovative technologies like artificial intelligence, robotics, and smart devices. These technological advancements are creating entirely new solutions across healthcare analytics, consumer healthcare, medical devices, and drug discovery — all while maintaining robust data protection standards essential for patient privacy and security.

HEAL offers investors a vehicle to target this opportunity by providing exposure to companies that operate at the intersection of healthcare and technology sectors. As the healthtech landscape evolves, HEAL offers access to innovations that may deliver growth potential while addressing important healthcare challenges.

Footnotes

1. Global X ETFs forecast as of March 14, 2025 based on information derived from: Bloomberg Intelligence. (2024, August 14). Generative-AI Revenue Potential. Evaluate Pharma. (n.d.). Cardiovascular Monitoring & Diagnostic Devices WW Consensus Sales Forecasts. Accessed on March 11, 2025. Evaluate Pharma. (n.d.). Diabetic Care WW Consensus Sales Forecasts. Accessed on March 11, 2025. Evaluate Pharma. (n.d.). Non-Invasive Monitoring Devices WW Consensus Sales Forecasts. Accessed on March 11, 2025. Grand View Research. (2023). Clinical Trial Management Systems Market Size. Grand View Research. (2023). Insuretech Market Size. Grand View Research. (2024). Telemedicine Market Size. Insight Partners. (2023). Medical Scheduling Software Market Size. Markets and Markets. (2024). Surgical Robots Market Size. Markets and Markets. (2025, March). Pharmacy Automation Market Size. Precedence Research. (2023). Revenue Cycle Management Market Size. Precedence Research. (2024). Electronic Health Records Market Size. Statista. (2024). Online Pharmacy.

2. Ibid.

3. Global X ETFs analysis based on information derived from: FDA. (August 7, 2024). Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AI/ML)-Enabled Medical Devices.

4. PhRMA. (2021, September 20). Industry Profile 2021.

5. Journal of the American Medical Association. (2020, March 3). Estimated Research and Development Investment Needed to Bring a New Medicine to Market, 2009-2018.

6. JACC. (2019, June 24). Phase II Trials in Drug Development and Adaptive Trial Design.

7. Colangelo, M. (2019, September 3). For the First Time, AI Designs and Validates New Drug Candidate in Days.

8. Morgan Stanley. (2022, September 9). Why Artificial Intelligence Could Speed Drug Discovery.

9. Bloomberg Intelligence. (2024, August 14). Generative-AI Revenue Potential.

10. NVIDIA. (2024, March 5). TD Cowen’s 44th Annual Health Care Conference.

11. Global X ETFs analysis based on information derived from: FDA. (August 7, 2024). Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AI/ML)-Enabled Medical Devices.

12. MedTech Drive. (2022, September 21). Abbott’s Freestyle Libre linked to 67% drop in hospitalizations for type 2 diabetes patients: Study.

13. Cleveland Clinic. (2024, April 30). Robotic Surgery.

14. Intuitive Surgical. (2024, October 18). Quarterly Report, 2024 Q3.

15. Intuitive Surgical. (2025, January 23). 2024 Annual Report.

16. Omnicell. (2022, September 20). Omnicell Investor Day Presentation.

data.title: Realty Income: T-Bill Returns With More Risk Is A Mediocre Mix
data.description:
Oh, is this one going to ruffle some feathers! Or should I say "O" since I'm talking about Realty Income (NYSE:O), the self-proclaimed "Monthly Dividend Company." The company has a well-earned reputation as a solid operator and industry leader. That monthly dividend has made it a fan favorite on this platform. Which is why my take on it might be met with more jeers than cheers. But hey, someone has to be the recipient of all of that vitriol, so it might as well be me.

Why would I doubt the allure of a monthly dividend REIT, one of the top 10 US publicly traded real estate companies, with a straightforward "triple net lease" structure? After all, the company has so much of what I like. Profitability, outstanding growth within its sector and a valuation grade that is above average. That, at a time when it seems every blue-chip stock I follow has a D or F valuation grade.

I'll finish our take on the business, then bottom-line the reason while I'd not dissuade any investor who studied O and buys it or owns it already from keeping it in their portfolio, I simply think I can do better. Or at least, the same, using a REIT ETF.

Again, there's nothing glaring about the business here that I dislike. But as I have and will continue to do here, I will try to raise investor awareness about how few stocks as a percentage of the US market actually add alpha versus and ETF. At least, at this stage of the market cycle. I came to this conclusion the hard way, by finding over and over in my own investing that too often, the gains I had on stocks were in sync or inferior to what would have happened if I had...pardon the expression...ETF'd it instead.

My goodness, is that a great tenant roster!

Most of its tenants are very well known by investors and are also very consolidated in their markets, such as retailers like Dollar General (DG), 7-Eleven (OTCPK:SVNDY), or other companies focused on experience and other face-to-face needs like Lifetime Fitness and FedEx (FDX).

Sure, there's macroeconomic sensitivity in that group. However, the tenant base is generally made up of strong businesses, focused on services or relatively essential services. Although very mature, Realty Income continues to invest in new verticals, such as gaming, data centers and international expansion, supporting a better outlook for continued growth in the coming decades through this TAM expansion and efficient capital allocation.

Realty Income's balance sheet is also quite strong, with long and well-distributed debt over the next decade and a leverage (net debt/adj. EBITDARE) of 5.4x

But what shines through is the company's track record, as shown in the image below. Realty Income has managed to consistently advance the growth of its AFFO/share, which is basically a proxy for shareholder value, i.e. how much cash its operations are generating, and this will consequently impact the dividends distributed. The CAGR is around 5%, as can be seen in the chart below.

Solid Margin Of Safety, But Could Be Better

The chart below shows how Realty Income has managed to generate shareholder value. One part is through a high dividend yield, and another is through AFFO/share growth.

I estimate that the optimal point to buy Realty Income is around a 6% forward dividend yield. Currently, the price-to-FFO of 13x and the forward dividend yield of 5.5% are already quite attractive for a market short of good alternative stocks with a high dividend yield and high quality.

These figures are enough to sustain an exposure to the REIT, but they are not that great. The $3.22 dividend forward estimate per stock takes us to this dividend yield of 5.5%. When the REIT was at $53, it was possible to capture a 6.08% dividend yield with the same assumptions, a level closer to the historical one and which allowed for an even better margin of safety.

The yield is close to its 7-year-high, so this is a stock that I would start to consider as a buy in my Yield At a Reasonable Price (YARP) methodology. But the stock is somewhat range bound. So if I'm OK with a 5-6% yield and potential price volatility, it's a "go." But I'm not, so it isn't a go.

Can't I change my mind on this popular yield stock?

Here's where the rubber meets the road for me. As I've noted in the past, I strongly believe the markets' functionality shifted dramatically starting in the first full year after the pandemic began. So, the start of 2021. Since that time, about 4 years, ago, O has seen its 3-year annualized return drop from a Covid-recovery high of 16% to below zero. Does that make a "screaming buy?" Not to me. It makes it an "if I have to own a REIT and not a REIT ETF" buy. But as shown below, this is not just a one timeframe issue.

Those rolling periods of 3 years each have seen O underperform both the cap weighted and equal weighted REIT sector ETFs far more often than not. And nearly continuously for a few years. Of course, this can change. But remember, this has been a period of generally good operating results for O. This is not a company-specific problem, it is the modern markets at work.

Because for every self-directed investor that enjoys the feeling of getting that monthly paycheck from being an O shareholder, there is a massive amount of index fund money (in S&P 500 ETFs that own REITs, and in REIT-focused ETFs) that does not care what it is buying. It just buys the index! That's a big reason in my view why O and many other REITs don't outperform as easily as they used to.

Here is the same type of chart, but using a different REIT index, since the pair I used above do not go back far enough in time. This is a very different look to me. I can see why so many income investors became enamored with O.

But now, monthly income is available in many forms, via ETFs. Without stock specific risk. Since that post-pandemic period began at the start of 2021, O has been neck and neck with T-bills. I think it will likely outperform T-bills the next 5 years, but not by such a wide margin that I'd assume the risk that comes with it.

This is actually a constructive chart. I could easily see O making a move up toward the $65 level, even to $75 over the next few years. That, plus that monthly dividend, is a solid double-digit percent return. But that doesn't make O a buy, just a hold. Because if that move were to occur, my suspicion is I could do better. My current REIT sector holding is Crown Castle (CCI), and I like American Tower (AMT) as well, but don't currently own it.

But those are different types of REITs, and my CCI position is collared with a put option purchased, and a call option sold against it. So that's a very different reward/risk tradeoff I've set up for myself.

Good company, decent stock, not a buy for me

So I land on a hold rating for O. That bores me as much as it probably bores the legion of fans that rave about this stock. I "get" the raving, just not the analysis that concludes that this stock is poised to become an alpha generator. That's what I'm looking for when I buy individual stocks in any sector.
